pattern: Fixing how many operations you use turns a search into arithmetic: subtracting the fixed offset exactly k times leaves a specific leftover target that must be expressible as exactly k powers of two.
hints: Every operation removes one power of two plus a fixed offset -- so if you decide up front how many operations, k, you will use, the total amount subtracted from the offset alone is fixed, leaving one specific leftover target that the powers of two alone must sum to.

For a chosen k, that leftover target must be reachable as a sum of exactly k powers of two with repeats allowed: the fewest such powers ever needed is its number of set bits, and the most is the target itself (by splitting any bit into two half-sized ones).

So test k = 1, 2, 3, ... in order (a small bound comfortably covers every solvable case): compute the leftover target for that k, and accept the first k whose set-bit count is at most k and which is itself at least k.

edge_inputs.description: solvable in a handful of operations
unsolvable, offset only ever increases the remaining value
offset is zero, target equals num1 itself
both values at the maximum magnitude, negative offset
both values at the maximum magnitude, positive offset (unsolvable)
num1 equal to num2, tempting to answer 1 but offset direction makes it unsolvable in one step

stress.description: random num1/num2 pairs across the full allowed ranges
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    num1 = rng.randint(1, 1000000000)
    num2 = rng.randint(-1000000000, 1000000000)
    return [num1, num2]
